questions.question: Explain the difference between fixed-function pipeline and programmable pipeline in computer graphics.
keyPoints: Fixed-function has hardcoded stages with limited/no customization
Programmable allows writing custom shaders for each stage
Programmable enables advanced effects like shadows, reflections, procedural textures
Fixed-function is obsolete, programmable is modern standard

questions.question: List and briefly describe the main programmable stages in the modern graphics pipeline.
keyPoints: Vertex Shader: transforms vertex positions, calculates per-vertex lighting
Geometry Shader: processes primitives, can create new geometry
Tessellation Shader: subdivides primitives for detail
Fragment Shader: determines pixel color and attributes
Compute Shader: general-purpose GPU computing (optional)

questions.question: What is rasterization? Explain its role in the programmable pipeline.
keyPoints: Converts geometric primitives into fragments
Samples each primitive to determine covered pixels
Generates interpolated values for fragment attributes
Comes after primitive assembly, before fragment shader
Essential step between vertex processing and fragment processing

questions.question: Explain the purpose and functions of a Vertex Shader.
keyPoints: Transforms vertex positions from model to clip space
Applies projection and view transformations
Calculates lighting values at vertices
Passes data to rasterizer via varyings/interpolators
Cannot create or destroy vertices, processes one at a time

questions.question: What is the difference between Vertex Shader and Fragment Shader?
keyPoints: Vertex shader processes vertices, Fragment shader processes fragments/pixels
Vertex shader determines geometry position, Fragment determines final color
Vertex runs once per vertex, Fragment runs once per pixel
Vertex outputs clip space positions, Fragment outputs color values
Fragment comes after rasterization, Vertex before it

questions.question: Describe what uniform variables are in shaders and their significance.
keyPoints: Global variables constant across all shader invocations
Set by CPU application before draw call
Used to pass application data to shaders (matrices, textures, time)
Remain unchanged during single draw call
Different from varying/attribute variables which change per vertex/fragment

questions.question: Explain the concept of Render-to-Texture and its applications.
keyPoints: Rendering scene output to a texture instead of screen
Enables post-processing effects (bloom, blur)
Used for shadow mapping and reflections
Supports multiple render targets (MRT)
Allows deferred rendering techniques

questions.question: Draw and explain the complete programmable graphics pipeline architecture.
keyPoints: Diagram showing: Input Assembler → Vertex Shader → (optional: Tessellation, Geometry) → Rasterization → Fragment Shader → Output Merger
Vertex Shader transforms vertices, performs per-vertex lighting
Geometry/Tessellation shaders for optional geometry manipulation
Rasterization converts primitives to fragments
Fragment shader determines fragment color with interpolation
Output merger handles blending, depth testing, stencil operations
Explain data flow between stages
Mention fixed-function vs programmable stages

questions.question: Explain different types of shaders and their specific purposes in the programmable pipeline.
keyPoints: Vertex Shader: vertex transformation and per-vertex lighting
Fragment/Pixel Shader: per-pixel color determination and effects
Geometry Shader: primitive manipulation and generation
Tessellation Shader: adaptive detail through subdivision
Compute Shader: general-purpose parallel computing
Compute requirements and limitations of each
Practical applications and use cases for each type

questions.question: Explain the graphics memory organization and buffer management in programmable pipelines.
keyPoints: Vertex Buffer Objects (VBOs) store vertex data
Index Buffers store primitive indices
Uniform Buffers for shared shader data
Framebuffers contain color, depth, stencil attachments
Render targets and framebuffer objects (FBOs)
Texture memory for sampled data
Double buffering and swap chains for display

questions.question: Discuss optimization techniques used in programmable graphics pipelines.
keyPoints: Early depth testing to skip expensive fragment processing
Level of Detail (LOD) with tessellation
Frustum and occlusion culling
Batching draw calls to reduce CPU-GPU overhead
Shader LOD: simpler shaders for distant objects
Deferred rendering to reduce overdraw
Multi-threading command buffer generation
